examples.explanation: Graph: 0→1→2→3. Start at 0: visited[0]=T, DFS to 1: visited[1]=T, DFS to 2: visited[2]=T, DFS to 3: visited[3]=T, no neighbors, return False. Back to 2: no more neighbors, return False. Back to 1: no more, return False. Back to 0: no more, return False. No cycle found. Reset visited, start at 1: visited[1]=T, DFS to 2: visited[2]=T, DFS to 3: visited[3]=T, return False. Back to 2: no more, return False. Back to 1: no more, return False. Start at 2: visited[2]=T, DFS to 3: visited[3]=T, return False. Back to 2: no more, return False. Start at 3: visited[3]=T, no neighbors, return False. All starts return False, so no cycle.

Graph: 0→1→2→0 (cycle). Start at 0: visited[0]=T, DFS to 1: visited[1]=T, DFS to 2: visited[2]=T, DFS to 0: visited[0] is True (since visited[0] from start), return True immediately. Cycle found, so overall return True. Note: We don't need to check other starts since cycle already detected.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force DFS O(V*(V+E))
approaches.summary: For each vertex, perform a DFS to check if there is a cycle reachable from it, resetting visited states each time. This is straightforward but inefficient for large graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Detect Cycle in Directed Graph Using Brute Force DFS
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of cycle detection by performing DFS from each vertex with reset visited states.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires determining if a directed graph contains a cycle, which is crucial for applications like course scheduling where cycles represent impossible prerequisites. The brute force DFS approach works by treating each vertex as a potential starting point for a cycle. For each vertex v, we perform a DFS to explore all reachable vertices. If during this DFS we encounter a vertex that's already in the current recursion stack (meaning we've returned to a vertex we're currently exploring from v), we've found a cycle. The key inefficiency comes from resetting visited states for each starting vertex, causing redundant exploration. Intuition: A cycle exists if there's a path from some vertex back to itself through directed edges. By checking every possible starting point, we guarantee detection of any cycle, though at high computational cost. Step-by-step: 1. Build adjacency list from edge list. 2. For each vertex v from 0 to V-1: a. Initialize visited array of size V to False. b. Call DFS(v, visited). 3. In DFS(u, visited): Mark u as visited. For each neighbor w of u: If w is visited, return True (cycle found). Else if DFS(w, visited) returns True, propagate True. Return False if no cycle from u. 4. If any DFS call returns True, graph has cycle; else no cycle. Time: O(V*(V+E)) because for each of V vertices, we potentially explore all V vertices and E edges in worst case (complete graph). Space: O(V+E) for adjacency list plus O(V) for visited array. Connection to L1: This uses the DFS subroutine from L1 repeatedly. Common mistakes: Not resetting visited array for each starting vertex (would miss cycles not reachable from first DFS), confusing directed with undirected cycles, or using BFS which doesn't easily track recursion stack. Real code pattern: def has_cycle(V, edges): adj = [[] for _ in range(V)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v); for start in range(V): visited = [False]*V; if dfs(start, visited, adj): return True; return False; def dfs(u, visited, adj): visited[u]=True; for w in adj[u]: if visited[w]: return True; if dfs(w, visited, adj): return True; return False.

examples.trace: Graph: 0→1→2→3. Start at 0: visited[0]=T, DFS to 1: visited[1]=T, DFS to 2: visited[2]=T, DFS to 3: visited[3]=T, no neighbors, return False. Back to 2: no more neighbors, return False. Back to 1: no more, return False. Back to 0: no more, return False. No cycle found. Reset visited, start at 1: visited[1]=T, DFS to 2: visited[2]=T, DFS to 3: visited[3]=T, return False. Back to 2: no more, return False. Back to 1: no more, return False. Start at 2: visited[2]=T, DFS to 3: visited[3]=T, return False. Back to 2: no more, return False. Start at 3: visited[3]=T, no neighbors, return False. All starts return False, so no cycle.

Graph: 0→1→2→0 (cycle). Start at 0: visited[0]=T, DFS to 1: visited[1]=T, DFS to 2: visited[2]=T, DFS to 0: visited[0] is True (since visited[0] from start), return True immediately. Cycle found, so overall return True. Note: We don't need to check other starts since cycle already detected.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS from a Single Starting Vertex to Detect Cycle
ladders.desc: Perform DFS from a given vertex to check if any cycle is reachable, using a visited array.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the brute force approach: given a starting vertex, it explores all vertices reachable via directed edges to detect if a cycle exists in that reachable subgraph. Intuition: Starting from vertex u, we traverse outgoing edges recursively. If we ever encounter a vertex that has already been visited during this DFS (meaning we're currently exploring it or have explored it in this call chain), we've found a cycle because we've returned to a vertex along a directed path. The visited array tracks which vertices have been visited in the current DFS call stack. Step-by-step: 1. Input: current vertex u, visited array (size V, initially all False for this start), adjacency list. 2. Mark u as visited. 3. For each neighbor w of u: a. If visited[w] is True, return True (cycle detected). b. Else, recursively call DFS(w, visited, adj). If that returns True, propagate True. 4. If no cycle found from u, return False. Time: O(V+E) for exploring the reachable subgraph in worst case. Space: O(V) for visited array plus recursion stack O(V) in worst case. Connection to L0: This is called V times in L0, once per starting vertex. Common mistakes: Forgetting to mark u as visited before recursing (infinite recursion), not checking visited before recursing (redundant work), or using a global visited without resetting (incorrect for brute force). Real code pattern: def dfs(u, visited, adj): visited[u] = True; for w in adj[u]: if visited[w]: return True; if dfs(w, visited, adj): return True; return False.

examples.trace: Adj: 0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[]. visited=[F,F,F]. DFS(0): visited[0]=T. Neighbors: w=1, visited[1]=F, call DFS(1). DFS(1): visited[1]=T. Neighbors: w=2, visited[2]=F, call DFS(2). DFS(2): visited[2]=T, no neighbors, return False. Back to DFS(1): no more neighbors, return False. Back to DFS(0): no more neighbors, return False. No cycle found.

Adj: 0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[0]. visited=[F,F,F]. DFS(0): visited[0]=T. Neighbors: w=1, visited[1]=F, call DFS(1). DFS(1): visited[1]=T. Neighbors: w=2, visited[2]=F, call DFS(2). DFS(2): visited[2]=T. Neighbors: w=0, visited[0]=True (from DFS(0)), so return True immediately. Propagates back: DFS(2) returns True to DFS(1), which returns True to DFS(0), which returns True overall.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Detecting Cycle in DFS by Tracking Visited Vertices
ladders.desc: The insight that a cycle exists if DFS revisits a vertex already in the current recursion path.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique: during a DFS traversal, maintain a visited set that indicates which vertices are currently in the recursion stack. If we encounter a vertex that's already visited, it means we've looped back, indicating a cycle. Intuition: In a directed graph, a cycle is a closed path where you can start and end at the same vertex. During DFS, if we're exploring from vertex A and eventually reach A again via directed edges, that's a cycle. The visited array serves as memory of the current path. Step-by-step: 1. Start DFS from a vertex. 2. When entering a vertex u, mark it as visited. 3. For each neighbor w: a. If w is visited, cycle detected. b. Otherwise, recurse on w. 4. When leaving u, in standard cycle detection we might unmark, but in this brute force approach, we keep visited marked for the entire DFS call (since we're checking reachability from a single start). Time: O(V+E) for the DFS. Space: O(V) for visited. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the DFS subroutine. Common mistakes: Confusing with undirected graphs where cycles require different handling, or not distinguishing between visited in this DFS vs. globally visited. Real code pattern: visited = [False]*V; def dfs(u): visited[u]=True; for w in adj[u]: if visited[w]: return True; if dfs(w): return True; return False.

examples.trace: DFS(0): mark visited[0]=T. Go to neighbor 1: visited[1]=F, DFS(1): mark visited[1]=T. Go to neighbor 2: visited[2]=F, DFS(2): mark visited[2]=T. Go to neighbor 0: visited[0]=T (since visited[0] is True from start), so cycle detected. Return True.

DFS(0): mark visited[0]=T. Go to neighbor 1: visited[1]=F, DFS(1): mark visited[1]=T. Go to neighbor 2: visited[2]=F, DFS(2): mark visited[2]=T. No neighbors, return False. Back to DFS(1): no more neighbors, return False. Back to DFS(0): no more neighbors, return False. No visited vertex encountered twice, so no cycle.

testCases.input: Graph: single vertex 0 with self-loop, start at 0

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation and DFS Traversal
ladders.desc: Represent a directed graph using adjacency list and perform basic DFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves two parts: representing the graph and traversing it with DFS. For cycle detection, we need a directed graph representation that allows efficient neighbor access. Adjacency list is ideal: an array of lists where index u contains all vertices v such that there's an edge u→v. DFS traversal is a recursive algorithm that explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. Intuition: DFS mimics exploring a maze by always taking the first available path until dead end, then backtracking. For graphs, it helps explore all vertices reachable from a start. Step-by-step: 1. Build adjacency list from edge list: initialize empty list for each vertex, for each edge (u,v), append v to adj[u]. 2. DFS from start s: a. Mark s as visited. b. For each neighbor w in adj[s]: if w not visited, recursively call DFS(w). Time: O(V+E) to build adjacency list and traverse. Space: O(V+E) for adjacency list, O(V) for visited array. Connection to L2: This provides the structure and traversal method needed for cycle detection logic. Common mistakes: Using adjacency matrix (O(V²) space), forgetting to handle disconnected graphs, or infinite recursion if visited not marked. Real code pattern: adj = [[] for _ in range(V)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v); visited = [False]*V; def dfs(u): visited[u]=True; for w in adj[u]: if not visited[w]: dfs(w).

examples.trace: Build adj: 0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[]. visited=[F,F,F]. DFS(0): mark visited[0]=T. Neighbors: 1 not visited, call DFS(1). DFS(1): mark visited[1]=T. Neighbors: 2 not visited, call DFS(2). DFS(2): mark visited[2]=T, no neighbors, return. Back to DFS(1): done. Back to DFS(0): done. Visited vertices in order: 0,1,2.

examples.output: Traversal order: 0,1,3,2 or 0,2,1,3 depending on neighbor order
examples.trace: Build adj: 0:[1,2], 1:[3], 2:[], 3:[]. visited=[F,F,F,F]. DFS(0): mark visited[0]=T. Neighbors: assume order 1 then 2. For w=1: not visited, call DFS(1). DFS(1): mark visited[1]=T. Neighbors: 3 not visited, call DFS(3). DFS(3): mark visited[3]=T, no neighbors, return. Back to DFS(1): done. Back to DFS(0): next neighbor w=2: not visited, call DFS(2). DFS(2): mark visited[2]=T, no neighbors, return. Back to DFS(0): done. Order: 0,1,3,2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Graph Neighbors Using Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Access and process all outgoing neighbors of a vertex in a directed graph.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves, given a vertex u in a directed graph, retrieving all vertices v such that there's a directed edge from u to v, and then performing some action on each v (like visiting or checking). In adjacency list representation, this is done by accessing the list at index u in an array of lists. Intuition: Graphs represent relationships; neighbors are directly connected vertices. For directed graphs, we care about outgoing edges. Step-by-step: 1. Represent graph as adjacency list: array adj where adj[u] is a list of integers. 2. To iterate neighbors of u: for v in adj[u]: do something with v. Time: O(degree(u)) where degree(u) is number of outgoing edges from u. Space: O(1) beyond the adjacency list storage. Connection to L3: This is used within DFS traversal to explore from each vertex. Common mistakes: Confusing incoming and outgoing neighbors, iterating over wrong index, or modifying list while iterating. Real code pattern: adj = [[1,2], [3], []] for V=3; for u=0, neighbors are 1 and 2: for v in adj[0]: print(v) outputs 1 then 2.

examples.trace: adj[0] is list [1,2]. Iterate: first neighbor v=1, process it (e.g., print or visit). Next neighbor v=2, process it. Done.
adj[2] is list [0,1]. Iterate: v=0, process. v=1, process. No more neighbors.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graph and Vertex Representation
ladders.desc: Understand what a directed graph is and how vertices are labeled numerically.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: a directed graph consists of vertices (nodes) and directed edges (arrows) connecting them. Each edge goes from one vertex to another, indicating a one-way relationship. Vertices are typically labeled with integers from 0 to V-1 for V vertices. Intuition: Think of cities with one-way streets; you can go from city A to B but not necessarily back. In programming, we represent this with V as number of vertices and edges as pairs (u,v) meaning edge from u to v. Step-by-step: 1. Define V = number of vertices. 2. Define edges = list of pairs, e.g., [(0,1), (1,2)] means edge 0→1 and 1→2. 3. Visualize: vertex 0 points to 1, vertex 1 points to 2. Time/Space: Not applicable at this conceptual level. Connection to L4: This foundation is needed to build adjacency lists and iterate neighbors. Common mistakes: Confusing directed with undirected edges, or mislabeling vertices. Real code pattern: V = 3; edges = [(0,1), (1,2)]; this defines a graph with vertices 0,1,2 and edges as described.

examples.trace: Vertices: 0,1,2. Edge (0,1): from 0 to 1. Edge (1,2): from 1 to 2. No edge from 2 to anywhere. So graph is a chain: 0 points to 1, 1 points to 2.
Vertices: 0,1,2,3. Edge (0,1): from 0 to 1. Edge (2,3): from 2 to 3. No edges between other pairs. So two separate components: one with 0 and 1, another with 2 and 3.

testCases.expected: Graph: single vertex 0 with no edges

approaches.name: Approach 2: DFS with Color States O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Use a three-color system (unvisited, visiting, visited) during DFS to detect cycles by tracking recursion stack. This avoids redundant checks and is efficient for directed graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Course Schedule Feasibility with DFS Color States
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to detect cycles in a directed graph representing course prerequisites using three-color DFS.
ladders.explanation: This problem models course prerequisites as a directed graph where each course is a node and prerequisites are directed edges. A cycle indicates impossible prerequisites. The three-color DFS approach efficiently detects cycles by tracking node states: WHITE (unvisited), GRAY (currently in recursion stack/visiting), BLACK (fully processed). During DFS, if we encounter a GRAY node, we've found a cycle. The intuition is that GRAY nodes represent nodes we're currently exploring in the current DFS path - encountering one means we've returned to a node before finishing its processing, indicating a back edge/cycle. Step-by-step: 1) Build adjacency list from prerequisites. 2) Initialize all nodes as WHITE. 3) For each WHITE node, run DFS. 4) In DFS: mark node GRAY, recursively visit neighbors, if any neighbor is GRAY return false (cycle detected), after processing all neighbors mark node BLACK. 5) If any DFS returns false, overall result is false (cycle exists). Time: O(V+E) for building graph and DFS traversal. Space: O(V+E) for adjacency list and color array. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into a complete solution. Common mistakes: Not handling disconnected graphs (must check all nodes), forgetting to mark nodes BLACK after processing, incorrect cycle detection logic. Real pattern: def canFinish(numCourses, prerequisites): graph = [[] for _ in range(numCourses)]; for a,b in prerequisites: graph[b].append(a); color = [0]*numCourses; def dfs(node): if color[node]==1: return False; if color[node]==2: return True; color[node]=1; for nei in graph[node]: if not dfs(nei): return False; color[node]=2; return True; for i in range(numCourses): if color[i]==0: if not dfs(i): return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build graph: node0->[1], node1->[]. Step 2: Initialize colors: [0,0]. Step 3: Start DFS from node0 (WHITE). Step 4: Mark node0 GRAY. Step 5: Visit neighbor node1 (WHITE). Step 6: Mark node1 GRAY. Step 7: Node1 has no neighbors, mark BLACK, return true. Step 8: Back to node0, all neighbors processed, mark BLACK, return true. Step 9: All nodes visited, no cycles, return true.

Step 1: Build graph: node0->[1], node1->[0]. Step 2: Initialize colors: [0,0]. Step 3: Start DFS from node0 (WHITE). Step 4: Mark node0 GRAY. Step 5: Visit neighbor node1 (WHITE). Step 6: Mark node1 GRAY. Step 7: Visit neighbor node0 (GRAY) - cycle detected! Return false. Step 8: Propagation returns false overall.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS with Three-Color Cycle Detection
ladders.desc: Implement DFS function that uses three colors to detect cycles in a directed graph.
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive DFS function that traverses a directed graph while detecting cycles using three states: 0=WHITE (unvisited), 1=GRAY (currently in recursion stack), 2=BLACK (fully processed). The intuition: when we're exploring a path (nodes marked GRAY), if we encounter another GRAY node, we've found a cycle because we've returned to a node before finishing its processing. This is more efficient than tracking explicit recursion stack because colors provide O(1) cycle detection. Step-by-step: 1) Function takes current node and color array. 2) If node is GRAY (1), return false (cycle). 3) If node is BLACK (2), return true (already processed). 4) Mark node GRAY (1). 5) For each neighbor: recursively call DFS; if returns false, propagate false. 6) After processing all neighbors, mark node BLACK (2) and return true. Time: O(V+E) for DFS traversal. Space: O(V) for recursion stack and color array. Connection: This function is called repeatedly in L0 to process all nodes. Common mistakes: Not checking BLACK nodes early (causes re-processing), incorrect color values, not propagating cycle detection properly. Real pattern: def dfs(node, color, graph): if color[node]==1: return False; if color[node]==2: return True; color[node]=1; for nei in graph[node]: if not dfs(nei, color, graph): return False; color[node]=2; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Node0 WHITE, mark GRAY. Step 2: Visit neighbor node1 (WHITE). Step 3: Node1 mark GRAY. Step 4: Visit neighbor node2 (WHITE). Step 5: Node2 mark GRAY, no neighbors, mark BLACK, return true. Step 6: Back to node1, all neighbors processed, mark BLACK, return true. Step 7: Back to node0, all neighbors processed, mark BLACK, return true.
Step 1: Node0 WHITE, mark GRAY. Step 2: Visit neighbor node1 (WHITE). Step 3: Node1 mark GRAY. Step 4: Visit neighbor node0 (GRAY) - cycle detected! Return false. Step 5: Propagation returns false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Three-Color System for Cycle Detection
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the three-color state system to detect cycles during graph traversal.
ladders.explanation: The three-color system (WHITE=0/unvisited, GRAY=1/visiting, BLACK=2/visited) is a clever way to track node states during DFS to detect cycles in O(1) time. The intuition: when we start exploring a node, we mark it GRAY to indicate it's in the current recursion path. If during exploration we encounter another GRAY node, we've found a cycle because we've returned to a node before finishing its processing. After fully processing a node (all neighbors explored), we mark it BLACK to avoid re-processing. This is more efficient than maintaining an explicit stack or visited set because: 1) GRAY state implicitly represents the recursion stack, 2) BLACK state prevents redundant work, 3) Cycle detection is immediate when encountering GRAY. Step-by-step: Initialize all nodes WHITE. During DFS: if node is GRAY→cycle; if BLACK→skip; else mark GRAY, process neighbors, mark BLACK. Time: O(1) for state checks. Space: O(V) for color array. Connection: This logic is embedded in the L1 DFS function. Common mistakes: Using wrong color values, not resetting colors between tests, confusing GRAY and BLACK semantics. Real pattern: colors = [0]*n; def process(node): if colors[node]==1: return 'cycle'; if colors[node]==2: return 'skip'; colors[node]=1; # process neighbors; colors[node]=2.

examples.trace: Step 1: Process node0: WHITE→GRAY. Step 2: Process node1: WHITE→GRAY. Step 3: Process node2: WHITE→GRAY. Step 4: Process node3: WHITE→GRAY→BLACK. Step 5: Back to node2: GRAY→BLACK. Step 6: Back to node1: GRAY→BLACK. Step 7: Back to node0: GRAY→BLACK. All nodes become BLACK, no GRAY encounters.
Step 1: Process node0: WHITE→GRAY. Step 2: Process node1: WHITE→GRAY. Step 3: Process node2: WHITE→GRAY. Step 4: Process node0: Already GRAY! Cycle detected at this point.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation and DFS Traversal
ladders.desc: Create adjacency list from edge list and perform basic DFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on two fundamental operations: 1) Building an adjacency list representation of a directed graph from an edge list, and 2) Performing a basic DFS traversal. The adjacency list is efficient for DFS because it provides direct access to neighbors. For a directed graph, each edge (u,v) means u points to v. Step-by-step for building: Initialize empty list for each node. For each edge (u,v), append v to list[u]. For DFS traversal: Use recursion or stack to explore nodes. Basic DFS: mark node visited, recursively visit all unvisited neighbors. The intuition: DFS explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking, which is ideal for detecting paths and dependencies. Time: O(V+E) for building and traversal. Space: O(V+E) for adjacency list, O(V) for visited set. Connection: This provides the graph structure and traversal framework for L2's color system. Common mistakes: Incorrect edge direction, not handling disconnected graphs, stack overflow in deep recursion. Real pattern: n=numNodes; graph=[[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: graph[u].append(v); visited=[False]*n; def dfs(node): visited[node]=True; for nei in graph[node]: if not visited[nei]: dfs(nei).

examples.trace: Step 1: Build graph: node0->[1], node1->[2], node2->[]. Step 2: Initialize visited=[F,F,F]. Step 3: DFS(0): mark visited[0]=T, visit neighbor 1 (unvisited). Step 4: DFS(1): mark visited[1]=T, visit neighbor 2 (unvisited). Step 5: DFS(2): mark visited[2]=T, no neighbors. Step 6: Backtrack complete.

Step 1: Build graph: node0->[1,2], node1->[3], node2->[3], node3->[]. Step 2: visited=[F,F,F,F]. Step 3: DFS(0): mark T, visit neighbor 1. Step 4: DFS(1): mark T, visit neighbor 3. Step 5: DFS(3): mark T, no neighbors, back to 1, back to 0. Step 6: Visit neighbor 2 from node0: DFS(2): mark T, visit neighbor 3 (already visited, skip).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Representing and Iterating Over Directed Graphs
ladders.desc: Create adjacency list from pairs and iterate through node neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level covers how to represent a directed graph using an adjacency list and perform basic iteration over nodes and edges. An adjacency list is an array of lists, where index i contains a list of nodes that i points to. This representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs and allows quick access to neighbors. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize empty list for each node. 2) For each directed edge (u,v), append v to list[u]. 3) To iterate: loop through all nodes, and for each node, loop through its neighbor list. The intuition: this captures the 'directed' relationship where u must come before v. Time: O(V+E) to build and iterate. Space: O(V+E) for the adjacency list. Connection: This data structure is essential for L3's DFS traversal. Common mistakes: Using wrong index, appending to wrong list, not initializing all node lists. Real pattern: n=numNodes; graph=[[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: graph[u].append(v); for i in range(n): print(f'Node {i} neighbors: {graph[i]}').

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize graph=[[],[],[]]. Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: append 1 to graph[0] → [[1],[],[]]. Step 3: Process edge [1,2]: append 2 to graph[1] → [[1],[2],[]]. Step 4: Final graph: node0 points to [1], node1 points to [2], node2 points to [].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graphs and Node Relationships
ladders.desc: Understand directed graphs as collections of nodes with one-way relationships.
ladders.explanation: A directed graph consists of nodes (vertices) and directed edges (arrows) connecting them. Each edge has a direction: from a source node to a destination node. This represents asymmetric relationships like prerequisites (course A must come before course B), dependencies, or flows. Key concepts: 1) Nodes represent entities. 2) Directed edges represent one-way relationships. 3) A path is a sequence of nodes following edge directions. 4) A cycle is a path that starts and ends at the same node. The intuition: if A points to B, then A must come before B; if there's a cycle (A→B→C→A), it's impossible to order them. Visualize as arrows between points. Step-by-step: Given nodes 0,1,2 and edge 0→1, node 0 points to node 1. Edge 1→2 means 1 points to 2. This forms a chain 0→1→2. If we add 2→0, we get a cycle 0→1→2→0. Time/Space: Conceptual only. Connection: This is the fundamental model for L4's adjacency list. Common mistakes: Confusing directed with undirected, misunderstanding edge direction. Real pattern: Think of nodes as circles, edges as arrows. Example: courses as nodes, prerequisites as arrows from prerequisite to course.

examples.output: Linear chain: 0 points to 1, 1 points to 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Node 0 exists. Step 2: Node 1 exists. Step 3: Node 2 exists. Step 4: Edge 0→1: draw arrow from 0 to 1. Step 5: Edge 1→2: draw arrow from 1 to 2. Step 6: Result: a chain 0→1→2 with no cycles.
Step 1: Nodes 0,1,2 exist. Step 2: Edge 0→1: arrow 0 to 1. Step 3: Edge 1→2: arrow 1 to 2. Step 4: Edge 2→0: arrow 2 to 0. Step 5: Result: a cycle where you can go 0→1→2→0 and back to start.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Kahn's Algorithm (BFS) O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Perform topological sort using BFS by repeatedly removing nodes with zero indegree. If all nodes are processed, no cycle exists; otherwise, a cycle is detected.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Course Schedule Feasibility Using Kahn's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to detect cycles in a directed graph representing course prerequisites using Kahn's BFS topological sort.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete course schedule problem where we need to determine if all courses can be taken given prerequisites. We represent courses as nodes and prerequisites as directed edges (prerequisite → course). The problem reduces to detecting cycles in this directed graph. Kahn's algorithm works by repeatedly removing nodes with zero indegree (courses with no remaining prerequisites). We maintain an indegree array counting incoming edges for each node. We use a queue to process nodes with indegree zero. When we remove a node, we decrement indegree of its neighbors. If we successfully process all nodes, the topological sort is valid (no cycle). If some nodes remain unprocessed, a cycle exists. Intuition: In a DAG, there must be at least one node with no dependencies to start. By removing these and updating dependencies, we either process all nodes (DAG) or get stuck (cycle). Step-by-step: 1) Build adjacency list and indegree array from prerequisites. 2) Initialize queue with nodes having indegree zero. 3) While queue not empty: pop node, add to result, for each neighbor decrement indegree; if indegree becomes zero, enqueue neighbor. 4) Compare result size with total nodes. Time: O(V+E) for building structures and BFS traversal. Space: O(V+E) for adjacency list and indegree. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into the complete solution. Common mistakes: Not handling disconnected graphs properly, incorrect indegree calculation, or missing queue initialization. Real pattern: Use collections.deque for queue, list for adjacency, and array for indegree.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency: {0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[3]}, indegree: [0,1,1,1]. Step 2: Queue init: [0] (indegree 0). Step 3: Process 0: result=[0], neighbors [1]: indegree[1]=0, enqueue 1. Step 4: Process 1: result=[0,1], neighbors [2]: indegree[2]=0, enqueue 2. Step 5: Process 2: result=[0,1,2], neighbors [3]: indegree[3]=0, enqueue 3. Step 6: Process 3: result=[0,1,2,3]. Step 7: Result size 4 equals numCourses 4 → true.
Step 1: Adjacency: {1:[0], 2:[1], 0:[2]}, indegree: [1,1,1]. Step 2: Queue init: [] (no node with indegree 0). Step 3: Queue empty, result=[]. Step 4: Result size 0 ≠ 3 → false (cycle detected).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Processing of Zero Indegree Nodes
ladders.desc: Implement the BFS loop that repeatedly removes nodes with zero indegree and updates neighbor indegrees.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core engine of Kahn's algorithm. Given an adjacency list and indegree array, we perform BFS starting from all nodes with indegree zero. The intuition: In any directed acyclic graph (DAG), there exists at least one node with no incoming edges (indegree zero). By removing such nodes and updating the indegrees of their neighbors, we either process all nodes (proving no cycle) or get stuck (indicating a cycle). Step-by-step: 1) Initialize a queue with all nodes where indegree[node] == 0. 2) Initialize a result list to store processed nodes. 3) While queue is not empty: a) Dequeue a node. b) Add it to result. c) For each neighbor in adjacency[node]: decrement indegree[neighbor]; if indegree[neighbor] becomes 0, enqueue it. 4) Return the result list. Time: O(V+E) as each node and edge is processed once. Space: O(V) for queue and result. Connection: This sub-routine is called within the full solution after building the graph structures. Common mistakes: Forgetting to enqueue neighbors when indegree becomes zero, or using stack instead of queue (BFS vs DFS). Real pattern: Use deque for queue, iterate while queue, process neighbors with indegree updates.

examples.trace: Step 1: Queue init: [0] (indegree 0). Step 2: Process 0: result=[0], neighbors [1,2]: indegree[1]=0 → enqueue 1, indegree[2]=0 → enqueue 2. Step 3: Process 1: result=[0,1], neighbors [3]: indegree[3]=1. Step 4: Process 2: result=[0,1,2], neighbors [3]: indegree[3]=0 → enqueue 3. Step 5: Process 3: result=[0,1,2,3]. Step 6: Queue empty, return result.
Step 1: Queue init: [] (no indegree 0 nodes). Step 2: Queue empty, result=[]. Step 3: Return empty result.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Indegree Tracking and Zero Detection
ladders.desc: Maintain and update indegree counts to identify nodes ready for processing.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves tracking the indegree (number of incoming edges) for each node in a directed graph. The intuition: In topological sorting, we need to start with nodes that have no dependencies (indegree zero). By maintaining an indegree array, we can efficiently find these starting points and update counts as we remove nodes. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize an indegree array of size V with zeros. 2) For each edge (u→v) in the graph: increment indegree[v] by 1 (since v depends on u). 3) To find nodes with indegree zero: scan the array and collect indices where value is 0. 4) When processing a node u: for each neighbor v of u, decrement indegree[v] by 1; if indegree[v] becomes 0, it's now ready for processing. Time: O(V+E) for initialization and updates. Space: O(V) for the array. Connection: This logic feeds into the BFS sub-routine by providing the indegree array and updates. Common mistakes: Incorrectly incrementing/decrementing indegree, or not resetting indegree between tests. Real pattern: Use list of integers, update during graph building and BFS.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize indegree = [0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Edge [0,1]: indegree[1]++ → [0,1,0,0]. Step 3: Edge [0,2]: indegree[2]++ → [0,1,1,0]. Step 4: Edge [1,3]: indegree[3]++ → [0,1,1,1]. Step 5: Edge [2,3]: indegree[3]++ → [0,1,1,2]. Final indegree.
Step 1: Initialize [0,0,0]. Step 2: Edge [0,1]: indegree[1]++ → [0,1,0]. Step 3: Edge [1,2]: indegree[2]++ → [0,1,1]. Step 4: Edge [2,0]: indegree[0]++ → [1,1,1]. Final indegree.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Construct an adjacency list representation from a list of edges.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating an adjacency list, a fundamental graph representation where each node maps to a list of its outgoing neighbors. Intuition: For directed graphs, an adjacency list efficiently stores edges and allows quick access to neighbors during traversal. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize an empty dictionary or list of lists of size V. 2) For each edge (u, v) in the input list: add v to the list for node u (since edge goes from u to v). 3) Ensure all nodes from 0 to V-1 have an entry (even if empty list). Time: O(E) to process all edges. Space: O(V+E) to store the adjacency list. Connection: This feeds into higher levels by providing the graph structure for indegree calculation and BFS. Common mistakes: Not initializing entries for all nodes, or incorrectly adding edges (e.g., adding u to v's list for directed graph). Real pattern: Use defaultdict(list) or list of lists, append neighbors.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty dict. Step 2: Edge [0,1]: add 1 to list for 0 → {0:[1]}. Step 3: Edge [0,2]: add 2 to list for 0 → {0:[1,2]}. Step 4: Edge [1,3]: add 3 to list for 1 → {0:[1,2], 1:[3]}. Step 5: Edge [2,3]: add 3 to list for 2 → {0:[1,2], 1:[3], 2:[3]}. Step 6: Add empty list for node 3 → {0:[1,2], 1:[3], 2:[3], 3:[]}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Management for BFS
ladders.desc: Implement basic queue operations (enqueue, dequeue) and BFS iteration.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using a queue to manage nodes in a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) order, essential for Breadth-First Search (BFS). Intuition: BFS explores nodes level by level; a queue ensures we process nodes in the order they become available (indegree zero). Step-by-step: 1) Initialize an empty queue (e.g., using deque). 2) Enqueue: add node to the back of the queue. 3) Dequeue: remove and return node from the front of the queue. 4) BFS loop: while queue not empty: dequeue a node, process it, enqueue its neighbors if conditions met. Time: O(V) for queue operations if processing all nodes. Space: O(V) for queue storage. Connection: This operation is used in the BFS sub-routine to process nodes. Common mistakes: Using list pop(0) which is O(n), or incorrect loop termination. Real pattern: from collections import deque; queue = deque(); queue.append(); node = queue.popleft().

examples.trace: Step 1: Queue init: deque([0]). Step 2: Dequeue 0: processed=[0], enqueue neighbors [1,2] → queue=[1,2]. Step 3: Dequeue 1: processed=[0,1], enqueue neighbor [] → queue=[2]. Step 4: Dequeue 2: processed=[0,1,2], enqueue neighbor [] → queue empty. Done.
Step 1: Queue init: deque([]). Step 2: Queue empty, no processing. Done.

testCases.input: initialNodes = [0], neighborsFunc = lambda n: [] if n==0 else []
initialNodes = [0], neighborsFunc = lambda n: [1] if n==0 else []
initialNodes = [0], neighborsFunc = lambda n: [1,2] if n==0 else [3] if n==1 else []
initialNodes = [0], neighborsFunc = lambda n: [1] if n==0 else [0] if n==1 else []
initialNodes = [0,2], neighborsFunc = lambda n: [1] if n==0 else []
initialNodes = [0], neighborsFunc = lambda n: [1] if n==0 else [2] if n==1 else [3] if n==2 else []
initialNodes = [0,1], neighborsFunc = lambda n: [2] if n<2 else []
initialNodes = [0], neighborsFunc = lambda n: [1,2] if n==0 else [3] if n==1 else [3] if n==2 else []

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graphs and Edges
ladders.desc: Understand directed graphs, nodes, edges, and basic terminology.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces directed graphs, a set of nodes (vertices) connected by directed edges (arrows). Intuition: A directed graph represents relationships with direction, like prerequisites where course A must come before course B. Key terms: Node/vertex: an entity (e.g., course). Edge: a directed connection from one node to another (e.g., prerequisite relationship). Indegree: number of edges coming into a node. Outdegree: number of edges going out of a node. Cycle: a path that starts and ends at the same node, indicating circular dependency. Step-by-step: 1) Identify nodes (usually numbered 0 to V-1). 2) For each edge (u,v), understand it goes from u to v. 3) Visualize as arrows between points. Time/Space: Basic conceptual, no complexity. Connection: This underlies all higher levels by providing the graph model. Common mistakes: Confusing directed with undirected edges, or misinterpreting edge direction. Real pattern: Think of nodes as circles, edges as arrows; use lists or matrices to represent.

examples.trace: Step 1: Nodes: 0,1,2. Step 2: Edge [0,1]: arrow from 0 to 1. Step 3: Edge [1,2]: arrow from 1 to 2. Step 4: Visual: 0 points to 1, 1 points to 2, no cycles.
Step 1: Nodes: 0,1,2. Step 2: Edge [0,1]: 0→1. Step 3: Edge [1,2]: 1→2. Step 4: Edge [2,0]: 2→0. Step 5: Visual: cycle formed: 0 to 1 to 2 back to 0.
